segments.s052-g9: همش (hamash) here = 'everything' or 'all of it' introducing a nominal predicate. The clause lists three nouns in coordination (داد و دین و فرهی) as qualities; there is no copula because Persian often omits it in poetic nominal predication. The repeated هم before دین and فرهی is an emphatic/parallel marker meaning 'also'/'and likewise.'
segments.s052-g10: همش again = 'everything' (repetition for emphasis). تاج و هم تخت are two coordinated nouns ('crown and throne'); هم is the same emphatic/coordinate particle. شاهنشهی is an adjectival/relational modifier 'imperial' / 'of kingship' modifying تخت. No copula is present; this phrase continues the nominal assertion begun in the previous segment.
segments.s052-g11: همه = 'all' (subject). راستی راست is a tautological/poetic pairing of noun + adjective (or two related nouns) meaning 'uprightness / what is right'; such doubling is common in Persian poetry for emphasis. از بخت اوست is a prepositional phrase ('from his fate/fortune') followed by the archaic/poetic copula ست (shortened form of است) meaning 'is.'
segments.s052-g12: همه = 'all' again (subject). فر و زیبایی is a coordinated noun phrase 'glory and beauty.' The next token ی is the bound copula/ending (here listed as #هست) functioning as part of the predicate 'are' together with ست at the end. از تخت اوست = 'is from his throne' (prepositional source + pronoun + copula).
segments.s052-g13: رسیدم is past tense, first-person singular 'I arrived/reached' (verb stem رسید + -م). به marks direction/goal. خوبی is a descriptive noun/adjective 'the fair/good (land)' modifying the following place phrase. بتوران is a fused/prepositional form meaning 'to/into Turan' (قدیم/شاعری form), and زمین is the noun 'land.' The phrase is a single directional NP ('to the fair land of Turan').
segments.s052-g14: سپه (army) is the direct object/topic. برکشیدیم is past plural 'we raised/raised up/drew up' (برکشیدن) indicating mustering/raising the army. و connects coordinated predicates. جستیم here is past plural 'we sought' (in the sense 'sought, searched for') rather than 'leapt'; کین is the object 'vengeance/hatred.' The subject 'we' is implied for both verbs.
segments.s052-g15: سه modifies the noun جنگ to give 'three wars/battles.' گران is an adjective 'heavy/serious/fierce' modifying جنگ. کرده شد is a passive/periphrastic construction: کرده (past participle 'made/done') + شد (auxiliary 'became/happened') yielding 'were made / took place.' در سه روز is an adverbial time phrase 'in three days.'
segments.s052-g16: The correlative چه ... چه ... (here 'چه در شب چه در هور') means 'both ... and ... / whether ... or ...' marking two circumstances. در شب = 'by night.' در هور گیتی فروز = 'in the Hûr that lights the world' — a poetic phrase for daylight/the sun: هور 'splendor/light' modified by the adjectival phrase گیتی فروز 'world-illuminating.' فروز is an attributive adjective 'bright/illuminating.'
segments.s052-g17: Two parallel prepositional phrases introduced by the poetic preposition 'از' (from/through) mark the sources of what follows: 'از ایشان شبیخون' = 'a night-raid from them' and the coordinated 'از ماکمین' = 'from (an) ambush'. The order is preposition + agent/source + noun; the comma or verb is omitted here and supplied by the following verb 'کشیدیم'.
segments.s052-g18: 'کشیدیم' is 1st-person plural past (we) and here means 'we suffered/experienced' (not 'drew' physically). The coordinating 'و' links a second verb 'جستیم', which here means 'we sought/searched for'. The phrase 'هر گونه کین' (every kind of vengeance) is the object of 'جستیم' — 'we sought every sort of revenge.'
segments.s052-g19: 'شنیدم که' introduces a reported clause ('I heard that ...'). 'ساز شبیخون' is a genitive/compound noun ('the tune/signal of the night‑raid') with the head 'ساز' (tune/instrument) followed by its modifier. 'گرفت' here is past tense 3rd singular and means 'took up / seized' (they or it took up the signal), i.e. the signal was raised/taken up.
segments.s052-g20: The preposition 'ز' (poetic form of 'از', 'from/because of') introduces a causal phrase 'ز بیچارگی' ('from/through helplessness/despair'). 'بند افسون' is a noun phrase 'the bond/fetter of enchantment', and the final 'گرفت' is used in the sense 'was seized/taken' (passive/intransitive: 'was seized by the spell').
segments.s052-g21: 'کمین ساختم' is a verbal phrase with the object 'کمین' (ambush) placed before the 1st‑person past verb 'ساختم' ('I made/set up'). The prepositional sequence 'از پس پشت اوی' literally 'from after/behind his back' is a poetic way to say 'behind him/his back'; 'اوی' is an archaic possessive meaning 'his'.
segments.s052-g22: 'نماندم' is 1st‑person past negative/predicative ('there remained / was left' in sense of 'nothing remained'). The phrase 'به جز' means 'except', and 'باد در مشت اوی' is a noun phrase 'the wind in his fist' (اوی = 'his'), i.e. only wind remained in his grasp (an idiom meaning he grasped nothing).
segments.s052-g23: 'یکایک' is an adverb meaning 'one by one' or 'each in turn'. 'چو' (= 'when') introduces a temporal clause. 'از جنگ برگاشت روی' = 'he turned his face away from the battle' — 'برگاشت' is 3rd‑person past 'he turned/removed', and 'روی' ('face/direction') is the object indicating the direction turned.
segments.s052-g24: 'پی اندر گرفتم' is a verbal phrase meaning 'I took after him / I pursued' where 'پی' ('after') plus 'گرفتم' ('I took/seized') yields 'followed/pursued'. 'رسیدم' is 'I arrived/reached', and 'بدوی' is an archaic/adverbial form indicating running/hasty motion — so together 'رسیدم بدوی' = 'I reached (him) while running.'
segments.s052-g25: بخفتانش is a causative/past verb phrase (archaic poetic form) meaning 'I made him sleep' used here idiomatically to mean 'I put him out/put him to death'; the enclitic -ش is the 3rd-person object 'him'. بر نیزه places the direct object on top of the postposition 'بر' (on/upon) + noun (نیزه, 'spear'); بگذاشتم is the past tense 1st-person verb 'I placed/put'. The clause order is verb-final for Persian poetry but still shows object + location + verb: 'I (subject) put him on the spear.'
segments.s052-g26: به نیرو is an adverbial phrase 'by/with force' (به + noun used adverbially). ازان is a demonstrative/prepositional 'from that' referring back to the saddle; زینش is زین + -ش 'his saddle' (possessed noun). برداشتم is the past 1st-person 'I removed/took away'. Together: 'with force I removed that saddle from him.'
segments.s052-g27: بینداختم is the past 1st-person 'I threw/cast down' (بیـ- prefix + انداختن). چون functions here as a simile marker 'like/as'. یکی in this context means 'a certain / a' (indefinite) and introduces the comparator اژدها 'dragon' — so 'I threw him down like a dragon.'
segments.s052-g28: بریدم is the past 1st-person 'I cut' (here used transitively). سر + ش is 'his head' (head + enclitic pronoun). از is the preposition 'from' and تن is 'body'; the phrase is a simple verb clause 'I cut his head from his body.' بی‌بها is a postpositive adjective (followed the noun phrase) meaning 'without price / priceless' and comments on the value of the spoil.
segments.s052-g29: فرستادم is past 1st-person 'I sent'. اینک 'now' is a temporal adverb. به نزد is the dative/locative construction 'to the presence of / to' (به + نزد); نیا here functions as the recipient (a name or title). The whole phrase is 'I sent (it) now to Nia.'
segments.s052-g30: بسازم is the present/subjunctive or jussive-form of ساختن used here as 'I will have/make' or 'let me have (it) made'; کنون 'now' again marks time. سلم را uses را as the definite/object marker on سلم (the thing to be altered), and کیمیا is the goal/result 'alchemy/elixir.' Grammatically this expresses purpose: '(I sent it) to have the salm made into an elixir.'
segments.s052-g31: چنان چون is a formulaic pair 'thus / as' introducing a comparison or manner. سر ایرج شهریار is a genitive/attributive sequence: 'the head of Irāj, the king' (name + title). Word order is noun (سر) + possessor (ایرج) + apposition/title (شهریار).
segments.s052-g32: به تابوت زر اندر is a locative phrase 'into/into a gold coffin' (به + تابوت زر + اندر 'within/in'). افگند is the past 3rd-person singular 'he threw/cast'. خوار is an adjectival comment 'despising/lowly' describing the manner (i.e., he cast it contemptuously/treated it as worthless). The verb follows its locative phrase, as is common in poetic prose.
segments.s052-g1: به شاه آفریدون is a prepositional phrase meaning “to King Afrīdūn.” یکی نامه کرد is a compact poetic clause: یکی (‘a certain’/‘one’) qualifies نامه (‘letter’), and کرد is the past tense of کردن used here as the lexical verb “did/made/sent” — together ‘he made/sent a letter (to King Afrīdūn).’ Word order (object before کرد) is typical in poetic Persian.
segments.s052-g2: ز مشک و ز عنبر marks the material or perfume with the poetic preposition ز (here “with/of”), repeated for emphasis: “with musk and with ambergris.” سر خامه is a genitival/compound expression ‘the tip/top (سر) of the quill/pen (خامه).’ کرد governs the whole phrase and means ‘made/treated/dipped’ — i.e. he adorned or scented the pen‑tip with those substances. The preposition ز functions instrumentally here rather than strictly spatially.
segments.s052-g3: نخست is the adverb ‘first/at first.’ از جهان‌آفرین is a prepositional/epithet phrase ‘of the World‑Creator’ (جهان‌آفرین is a compound epithet). کرد یاد is the verb phrase where یاد (‘remembrance/mention’) combines with کردن (کرد) to form یاد کردن ‘to remember/to mention’; the order is inverted poetically (یاد follows کرد) but the meaning is ‘he mentioned/remembered the Creator.’
segments.s052-g4: This is a nominal list giving attributes: خداوند (the Lord) followed by nouns linked by و: خوبی (‘goodness’), پاکی (‘purity’), و داد (‘and justice’). داد here is a noun meaning ‘justice’ not the past tense of دادن. There is no copula; Persian commonly strings nouns to ascribe qualities to a title.
segments.s052-g5: سپاس از ... is a praise/thanks construction: سپاس (‘thanks’ or ‘praise’) + از introduces the beneficiary/target (‘from/of the World‑ruler’). جهاندار is an epithet ‘World‑ruler/sovereign’ and فریادرس is an appositive epithet ‘rescuer/succorer.’ This is a compact nominal phrase offering thanks to that figure.
segments.s052-g6: نگیرد is the negative present/imperative form meaning ‘does not take/seize’ (3rd person), placed before its objects by poetic inversion. به سختی (‘in hardship’) is an adverbial phrase; جز او (‘except him’) restricts the following noun phrase دست کس (‘the hand of anyone’). دست کس here is idiomatic: دست = ‘help/assistance’ and کس = ‘anyone’; the clause means ‘in hardship no one’s help is received except his.’
segments.s052-g7: دگر آفرین means ‘another praise’ or ‘again praise’ (دگر = ‘again/anew’; آفرین here = exclamation/praise). بر فریدون uses بر to mark the target of the praise (‘upon/to Fereydun’). برز is an epithet after the name meaning ‘high/exalted.’ Word order places the epithet after the name as is common in poetic diction.
segments.s052-g8: This is a parallel apposition: خداوند تاج و خداوند گرز repeats خداوند (‘lord/sovereign’) as an honorific title applied to two symbols of rulership, تاج (‘crown’) and گرز (‘mace’). There is no copula; the nouns are juxtaposed to ascribe these rulerships to Fereydun.
segments.s052-g41: گنه بس گران بود و پوزش نبرد is a compact past-tense clause: گنه (the noun 'sin') is the subject, بس گران is an emphatic adjectival phrase ('very/heavily grave') modifying گنه, and بود is the past copula 'was.' The coordinate و links the second clause: پوزش (object 'pardon/excuse') plus نبرد is a classical past negative construction meaning 'did not take/accept' (i.e. 'would accept no pardon').
segments.s052-g42: و دیگر که کین خواه او بود گرد: و links on to the previous clause; دیگر here functions as 'moreover/again' (adding a further fact). که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. کین خواه is a noun+verbal adjective sequence meaning 'one desiring vengeance' (کین = vengeance, خواه = wanting). او is the subject 'he' and بود is the past copula 'was.' گرد here is a small classical verbal/adverbial element meaning 'turned toward / bent on' (so the whole clause says 'he was bent on vengeance').
segments.s052-g43: بیامد فرستاده شوخ روی is a simple clause with the past verb بیامد 'came' at the front (typical in narrative), فرستاده is the noun 'messenger' as subject, and شوخ روی is a two-word adjective phrase 'merry-faced' (شوخ modifying روی 'face'), describing the messenger's appearance.
segments.s052-g44: سر تور بنهاد در پیش اوی: سر تور (literally 'Tūr's head' or 'the head of Tūr') is the direct object; بنهاد is a past tense of نهادن ('put/placed') meaning 'he placed'; در پیش = 'in front of / before' and اوی is an archaic clitic form referring back to 'him' ('before him'). The whole phrase means 'he placed Tūr's head before him.'
segments.s052-g45: فریدون همی بر منوچهر بر: فریدون is the subject (proper name). همی is the classical narrative/progressive particle often giving immediacy or continuity ('then/was just'). The repeated preposition بر governs what follows and here locates the action with respect to منوچهر ('upon/for Manuchehr'); the doubled بر is a poetic repetition used in classical Persian to mark the object or to add emphasis.
segments.s052-g46: یکی آفرین خواست از دادگر: یکی ('one') quantifies آفرین ('acclamation/praise'); خواست is the past tense of خواستن ('asked/wanted') taking آفرین as its object—'asked for one acclamation.' از introduces the source/agent 'from' and دادگر names 'the just one / the just ruler' as the person from whom the acclamation was requested.
segments.s052-g33: به نامه درون این سخن کرد یاد is a compact phrase meaning “(in) the letter he made this remembrance.” به نامه functions instrumentally (“by / in a letter”), درون specifies location (“inside/within”), and این سخنکرد یاد is literal: این سخن “these words/this statement” + کرد (did/made) with یاد (remembrance/commemoration) — together “he put/wrote this remembrance.” Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect but follows classical Persian patterns.
segments.s052-g34: هیونی برافگند برسان باد: هیونی is a descriptive agent “a savage/monster”; برافگند is a past/perfective verb here meaning roughly “cast/send forth” (poetic), برسان is the verb “to bring/deliver,” and باد is the optative particle “may (it be).” Put together: “(may) he send/dispatch a savage to deliver it.”
segments.s052-g35: فرستاده آمد رخی پر ز شرم = “a messenger came, (his) face full of shame.” فرستاده is the subject “messenger,” آمد is the simple past “came,” and the following noun phrase رخی پر ز شرم describes the messenger: رخی (a visage/face, poetic), پر (full of), ز + noun = “of/from” so ز شرم = “with shame.”
segments.s052-g36: دو چشم از فریدون پر از آب گرم = “his two eyes, because of Fereydun, were full of warm water (tears).” دو چشم is the quantified noun, the first از here indicates cause/relation (“because of / on account of Fereydun”), پر از is the fixed expression “full of,” and آب گرم = “warm water” used idiomatically for hot/tearing eyes.
segments.s052-g37: که چون برد خواهد سر شاه چین introduces a subordinate temporal clause: که (= “that/for/when”), چون here functions as a temporal “when,” and برد in this poetic ordering is the verb ‘to take/obtain’ (placed before the auxiliary); خواهد is the future auxiliary “will,” so the phrase amounts to “when he will take/obtain the head of the king of China.” سر شاه چین is the direct object phrase “the head of the king of China.”
segments.s052-g38: بریده بر شاه ایران‌زمین = “(it) severed / cut off, upon/before the king of the land of Iran.” بریده is a past participle/adjective “cut off, severed,” بر indicates placement or direction (“on/upon/ before”), and شاه ایران‌زمین names the recipient/scene — “the king of Iran.” The clause thus says the head will be severed and presented to the Iranian king.
segments.s052-g39: که فرزند‌گر سر بپیچید ز دین = a conditional clause “that/if a son should turn his head away from the faith.” که introduces the clause; فرزند‌گر is an archaic/formal term referring to offspring (here ‘a son’ or ‘child’); سر بپیچید is a classical/poetic verbal form meaning “turn his head / turn away” (بپیچید as a jussive/ subjunctive/modal form: “should turn”); ز دین = “from the faith / from duty.”
segments.s052-g40: پدر را بدو مهر افزون ز کین = “(as for) the father, toward him (the son) love is greater than hatred.” را marks پدر as the topical patient/owner (“as for the father”), بدو = “toward him/to him,” مهر = “love/affection,” افزون = “greater, more,” and ز کین = “than hatred” (a comparative using ز). The whole states that a father’s affection for a son exceeds his vengeance/hatred even if the son turns away.

sentences.s052-s5: Everything was justice and religion and nobility; everything was crown and the throne of kingship.
sentences.s052-s6: All uprightness is from his fortune; all splendour and beauty are from his throne.
sentences.s052-s7: I reached the fair land of Turan; we raised an army and sought vengeance.
sentences.s052-s8: Three fierce battles were fought in three days, both by night and in the world-illuminating daylight.
sentences.s052-s9: We suffered night-attacks from them and ambushes, and we sought every kind of vengeance.
sentences.s052-s10: I heard that the war‑signal for a night‑raid was taken up; from despair he was seized by the bond of enchantment.
sentences.s052-s11: I set an ambush behind his back; nothing remained except the wind in his fist.
sentences.s052-s12: One by one, when he turned his face away from the battle, I took after him and, running, I reached him.
sentences.s052-s13: I laid him—having rendered him unconscious—upon a spear, and with force I removed that saddle from him.
sentences.s052-s14: I hurled him down like a dragon, and I cut off his head from his body — a priceless spoil.
sentences.s052-s15: I now sent it to Nia, to have (or order) him make the salm into an elixir.
sentences.s052-s16: Thus, like that, he cast the head of Irāj the king into a gold coffin, contemptuously.
sentences.s052-s1: He sent a letter to King Afrīdūn, using a pen‑tip scented with musk and ambergris.
sentences.s052-s2: First he made mention of the World‑Creator: ‘God — (the) lord of goodness, purity, and justice.’
sentences.s052-s3: Praise to the World‑Ruler, the Rescuer; in hardship no one's hand takes hold except his.
sentences.s052-s4: Again, he praised Fereydun the Exalted — lord of the crown and lord of the mace.
sentences.s052-s21: The sin was very heavy and he would accept no pardon; moreover he was bent on vengeance.
sentences.s052-s22: A merry-faced messenger came and placed Tūr's head before him.
sentences.s052-s23: Fereydun then asked for one acclamation for Manuchehr from the just ruler.
sentences.s052-s17: In that letter he wrote this remembrance; he sent a savage to carry it — may it be delivered.
sentences.s052-s18: A messenger arrived, his face full of shame and his two eyes filled with warm tears for Fereydun.
sentences.s052-s19: For when he will take the head of the king of China, it will be severed and brought to the king of the land of Iran.
sentences.s052-s20: That if a son should turn his head away from the faith, a father's love for him is greater than his hatred.
